reasoning: The sole gate protecting mtnc_register_settings() is check_admin_referer('mtnc_edit_post', 'mtnc_nonce'), which performs a cryptographic CSRF check only. There is no current_user_can('manage_options') call inside the handler. WordPress nonces are user-scoped, not role-scoped: any authenticated user (Subscriber, Contributor, Author, Editor) can call wp_create_nonce('mtnc_edit_post') from JavaScript or a direct API call and obtain a valid nonce for themselves. check_admin_referer() validates only that the nonce is cryptographically correct for the submitting user and action string — it does not verify that the user holds any capability. The nonce field in the settings page HTML (line 329: wp_nonce_field('mtnc_edit_post', 'mtnc_nonce')) is rendered only for manage_options users, but the low-privilege attacker does not need to view that page to obtain a nonce — they self-generate one. admin_init fires for every authenticated wp-admin request, so mtnc_register_settings() is registered and reachable when the attacker POSTs to any admin.php URL with their self-generated nonce and lib_options[] fields. Upon valid nonce + non-empty $_POST['lib_options'], update_option('maintenance_options', $lib_options) executes unconditionally (line 111), allowing a low-privileged authenticated user to overwrite all maintenance plugin settings including toggling maintenance mode on to lock out front-end visitors. The attack requires a logged-in account of any role; unauthenticated users cannot reach this because all AJAX handlers use wp_ajax_* (not wp_ajax_nopriv_*) and the admin_init hook requires an authenticated session. Severity remains MEDIUM (not elevated) because the impact is limited to overwriting plugin settings rather than RCE or data exfiltration.
